qualitative_claim: An investment may only proceed to analysis if the analyst can explain in plain language how the business makes money, who its customers are, and why those customers will continue to buy
qualitative_rule: Reject any investment where the analyst cannot articulate — without jargon, technology trend extrapolation, or industry-insider assumptions — the business model, the source of customer demand, and a plausible long-run trajectory
anti_scope: Does not prohibit investment in complex industries; prohibits investment in any business where the analyst's understanding is shallow or borrowed rather than genuine
rationale: Buffett's circle of competence is self-defined and defended with unusual rigidity — he passed on entire sectors including technology and biotech under heavy social pressure. Genuine understanding is operationally defined as the ability to explain the business simply and predict its state a decade out with real confidence.

qualitative_claim: A target business must possess an identifiable structural competitive advantage that protects its returns on capital from competitive erosion
qualitative_rule: Reject businesses where no identifiable source of durable competitive advantage — brand power, cost leadership, network effects, switching costs, or regulatory barriers — can be articulated and supported by observable business economics
anti_scope: Does not require the moat to be impenetrable or permanent; requires only that a meaningful structural advantage exists and can be identified with specificity
rationale: Moat analysis is the single most important qualitative factor in Buffett's mature framework. Without a structural competitive advantage, high returns on capital will be competed away over time, destroying the foundation for long-term compounding.

qualitative_claim: A competitive moat that is demonstrably narrowing on a secular basis is a disqualifying condition, even if the moat currently appears wide
qualitative_rule: If evidence shows the competitive position has been systematically losing ground — through declining market share, pricing power erosion, or successful competitive encroachment confirmed across multiple years — reject the investment regardless of the apparent current moat width
anti_scope: Does not apply to temporary, cyclical competitive pressure; applies only to structural, secular moat erosion confirmed over a multi-year period and supported by a credible mechanism of disruption
rationale: Buffett's largest analytical failures — newspapers and IBM — involved moats that were real at purchase but eroded faster than expected. The critical diagnostic is not whether the moat exists today but whether it is widening or narrowing. A real but narrowing moat is a liability, not an asset, over a long holding horizon.

qualitative_claim: The ability to raise prices without losing meaningful customer volume is the primary operational litmus test of whether a real economic moat exists
qualitative_rule: Assess whether the business has historically raised prices at or above input cost inflation while maintaining or growing unit volume over a full business cycle; if structural pricing power is absent, any moat claim requires extraordinary additional justification
anti_scope: Does not require prices to rise every year; assesses the structural capacity to raise prices across a cycle without triggering material volume destruction
rationale: Buffett stated to the Financial Crisis Inquiry Commission in 2010 that pricing power is more important than good management. He considers it the definitive operational test of whether a competitive moat is real. Coca-Cola's demonstrated ability to raise prices globally while sustaining volume was central to his 1988 investment thesis.

qualitative_claim: Management that lacks verifiable integrity is disqualifying regardless of business quality or valuation attractiveness
qualitative_rule: If management has a documented history of misleading shareholders, manipulating reported figures, or structuring transactions to benefit insiders at owner expense, reject the investment and do not reconsider on the basis of other merits
anti_scope: Does not apply to operational errors, poor capital allocation decisions, or strategic mistakes; applies specifically and exclusively to integrity failures and demonstrated dishonesty toward owners
rationale: Buffett has stated that intelligence and energy without integrity will destroy you. The Tesco accounting fraud and Salomon Brothers misconduct cases demonstrate this as a hard veto. Dishonest management converts what appears to be a business asset into a concealed liability that is typically discovered at the worst possible moment.

qualitative_claim: Management that retains earnings without creating equivalent or greater market value over time is destroying shareholder capital through deficient capital allocation
qualitative_rule: For every dollar of earnings cumulatively retained over a multi-year period, at least one dollar of incremental market capitalization should have been created; a persistent shortfall indicates management should be returning capital to owners rather than retaining it
anti_scope: Does not apply over short measurement windows or at cyclical troughs; applies to multi-year cumulative patterns only and is a diagnostic flag requiring further investigation, not an automatic rejection trigger
rationale: Buffett's retained earnings test is his specific, actionable capital allocation quality filter — it directly connects management decision-making to shareholder outcomes and operationalizes the distinction between value-creating retention and value-destroying empire-building.

qualitative_claim: The investment entity must not use conventional borrowed money that creates exposure to margin calls, fixed maturity dates, or forced liquidation
qualitative_rule: Prohibit structural use of margin accounts, leveraged instruments, or debt-financed investment positions at the portfolio entity level; any capital structure element that can compel selling at unfavorable prices is categorically excluded
anti_scope: Does not prohibit portfolio companies from carrying operational debt appropriate to their business; does not prohibit float from insurance operations; restricts only leverage at the investment entity level that introduces forced-sale risk
rationale: Buffett views conventional debt as structurally fragile — it introduces margin calls, maturity dates, and forced liquidation risk at precisely the worst times. The float model achieves leverage-like return amplification without this fragility. This explains what otherwise appears contradictory: constant warnings against leverage from a man running one of the most leveraged capital structures in finance.

qualitative_claim: Selling a position requires affirmative, articulable justification; the default state for a high-conviction holding is to hold indefinitely
qualitative_rule: A sell decision must be justified by at least one of: (a) confirmed secular deterioration of the business's competitive moat, (b) discovered management integrity failure, (c) a materially superior capital redeployment opportunity that exceeds the full tax cost of sale, or (d) price at a gross premium to any reasonable intrinsic value estimate; the absence of a strong fresh buy thesis alone does not constitute justification for selling
anti_scope: Does not mean positions can never be sold; establishes that selling is the exception requiring explicit cause, not a default response to price weakness, macro uncertainty, or peer pressure
rationale: Buffett's stated holding period is 'forever.' Truly great businesses become more valuable compounding over time. He has held Coca-Cola for over three decades including through periods of meaningful overvaluation, and has explained that the franchise's continuity made patience economically rational.

qualitative_claim: The permanent compounding cost of realizing a capital gain must be explicitly weighed before any sell decision on a position with significant embedded unrealized gains
qualitative_rule: Before finalizing a sell recommendation on a position with material unrealized gains, estimate the after-tax compounding base permanently lost to the tax event and compare it to the expected incremental benefit from redeployment; proceed only if the redeployment benefit materially and durably exceeds this permanent cost
anti_scope: Does not apply to positions with minimal embedded gains or to loss positions; does not restrict tax-loss harvesting; applies specifically to positions where capital gains taxation would represent a material permanent reduction in the long-run compounding base
rationale: Buffett has held Coca-Cola through periods of overvaluation explicitly because the tax cost of selling and rebuying would destroy more long-run value than patience costs. After-tax compounding math pervades his entire approach and distinguishes him from investors who optimize on a pre-tax basis.

qualitative_claim: Business value must be assessed on owner earnings rather than on reported accounting earnings
qualitative_rule: Define owner earnings as reported net income plus depreciation and amortization, minus the maintenance capital expenditures required to sustain competitive position and unit volume; use this figure as the primary earnings base for all valuation work, not GAAP net income
anti_scope: Does not prohibit using reported earnings as a starting input; requires adjustment for maintenance capex to arrive at the economically meaningful owner earnings figure before any valuation conclusion is drawn
rationale: Buffett defined owner earnings in his 1986 shareholder letter as the true measure of a business's cash-generation capacity. GAAP earnings can diverge materially from owner earnings in capital-intensive businesses. The Coca-Cola case is instructive: its asset-light model meant virtually all earnings growth translated directly to free cash flow, a distinction invisible in GAAP income.

qualitative_claim: Formal multi-year DCF spreadsheet models must not serve as the primary source of valuation conviction
qualitative_rule: Primary valuation must be conducted as a mental model: estimate normalized owner earnings, assess a conservative long-run growth rate, compare the implied purchase-price return against the required return threshold; a formal spreadsheet with multi-year projections, explicit terminal value, and WACC is prohibited as the primary valuation method
anti_scope: Does not prohibit quantitative cross-checking or sanity validation; prohibits treating spreadsheet model output as the primary source of conviction rather than as a check on mental reasoning
rationale: Buffett has explicitly stated he does not build multi-year projections with terminal values and WACC. Precision must reside in the quality of thinking, not in the false confidence of spreadsheet arithmetic — a model producing precise wrong answers is worse than a rough correct one.

qualitative_claim: Full qualitative assessment of business quality and competitive durability must be completed and confirmed before examining whether the price is attractive
qualitative_rule: Complete the business quality screens — circle of competence, moat existence, moat direction, pricing power, management integrity — before examining price multiples or valuation ratios; a business that fails qualitative screens must not be reconsidered solely because its price appears low
anti_scope: Does not mean price is unimportant or that it is the final consideration; means that price analysis is only valid and meaningful after business quality has been affirmatively confirmed
rationale: Buffett's Rule 6 states explicitly: lead analysis with business quality, not price. His evolution from Graham's cigar-butt approach was precisely the recognition that a wonderful company at a fair price beats a fair company at a wonderful price — a cheap price cannot rescue a structurally weak business over a long holding period.

qualitative_claim: A company should pay dividends only when it demonstrably cannot deploy retained capital at returns exceeding what owners could earn independently
qualitative_rule: Evaluate whether a dividend-paying company has genuinely exhausted high-return reinvestment opportunities; if the company is simultaneously paying regular dividends and earning high incremental returns on internally deployed capital, the capital allocation is sub-optimal and signals potential principal-agent misalignment
anti_scope: Does not disqualify dividend-paying companies from the investment universe; means dividend payment should be read as evidence about whether the business has run out of superior capital deployment options, not as a positive signal in itself
rationale: Buffett's capital allocation hierarchy places dividends as the last resort. His reasoning: if management can reinvest at returns exceeding shareholder opportunity cost, retained earnings compound faster inside the business. Berkshire has never paid a regular dividend because this condition has never been met.

qualitative_claim: The purchase price must imply an acceptable return even under a materially conservative, downside-case earnings scenario, not only under the base case
qualitative_rule: Run the valuation with a growth rate materially below the base case and confirm that the implied return at the purchase price remains acceptable relative to the risk-free alternative; if the investment thesis breaks under a plausible downside scenario, the price does not embed sufficient margin of safety
anti_scope: Does not require discounting all investments to Graham-era deep value levels; does not prohibit paying above-average multiples for exceptional businesses; requires only that the downside-case return remains acceptable before committing capital
rationale: Even within Buffett's evolved framework — where business quality provides a second layer of protection — there is always a price at which even the greatest business becomes a bad investment. In the Coca-Cola case, he specifically verified that a downside growth scenario still implied attractive returns at his purchase price before committing.

qualitative_claim: Management that demonstrably resists the institutional imperative — willing to hold excess cash, decline available acquisitions, and defy peer behavior when economics do not justify action — is a significant positive quality signal
qualitative_rule: Assess whether management has a documented pattern of declining available acquisitions when returns are insufficient, returning excess capital when no superior use exists, and resisting imitative pressure from industry peers; positive evidence includes past instances where management chose deliberate inaction over the pressure to deploy capital
anti_scope: Does not penalize management for making acquisitions per se; assesses specifically whether the pattern shows resistance to empire-building and peer imitation pressure as distinct from genuine opportunity-driven deployment
rationale: Buffett identified the institutional imperative — organizations' tendency to absorb available capital regardless of returns and reflexively imitate peers — as one of the most insidious forces in corporate governance. Managers who resist it are rare and command a meaningful quality premium in his analysis.
